questions.text: Which Mughal Emperor's death in 1707 marked the beginning of the decline of the empire?
questions.explanation: Aurangzeb's long war in the Deccan exhausted the military and financial resources of the empire.

questions.text: The 'Sayyid Brothers' were known as ________ during the later Mughal period.
questions.explanation: They controlled several emperors and wielded significant power in the court.

questions.text: Nadir Shah, the ruler of Iran, invaded Delhi in:
questions.explanation: He plundered the city and took away immense wealth, including the Peacock Throne.

questions.text: Who was the Afghan ruler who invaded North India five times between 1748 and 1761?
questions.explanation: His invasions further weakened the already fragile Mughal Empire.

questions.text: Burhan-ul-Mulk Sa'adat Khan was the founder of which state?
questions.explanation: He was appointed subadar of Awadh in 1722 and built a strong state.

questions.text: The state of Hyderabad was founded by:
questions.explanation: Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah was one of the most powerful members at the court of Emperor Farrukh Siyar.

questions.text: The revenue farmers in Awadh were known as:
questions.explanation: The state sold the right to collect tax to these highest bidders.

questions.text: Murshid Quli Khan broke the Mughal control in Bengal by:
options: Transferring all Mughal jagirdars to Orissa
questions.explanation: He ordered a major reassessment of the revenues of Bengal to secure his power.

questions.text: Which bank became extremely prosperous in Bengal during the reign of Alivardi Khan?
questions.explanation: The banking house of Jagat Seth became influential due to the state's dependence on them for revenue.

questions.text: The 'Watan Jagirs' of the Rajputs were:
options: Ancestral estates enjoyed without much Mughal interference
questions.explanation: Many Rajput kings, especially of Amber and Jodhpur, held these high positions under the Mughals.

questions.text: Raja Sawai Jai Singh of Amber founded his new capital at:
questions.explanation: He was given the subadari of Agra in 1722.

questions.text: Under whose leadership did the Sikhs organize themselves into a political community?
questions.explanation: He inspired the Khalsa with the belief that their destiny was to rule (Raj Karega Khalsa).

questions.text: The 'Rakhi' system introduced by the Sikhs offered protection to cultivators in exchange for a tax of:
questions.explanation: This system helped the Sikhs consolidate their power over a large territory.

questions.text: The combined grand army of the Sikhs was known as:
questions.explanation: The Dal Khalsa used to meet at Amritsar during Baisakhi and Diwali.

questions.text: Who was the Sikh leader who reunited the various misls and established his capital at Lahore in 1799?
questions.explanation: He was a powerful ruler who expanded the Sikh kingdom significantly.

questions.text: Shivaji carved out a stable kingdom with the support of powerful warrior families called:
questions.explanation: Deshmukhs were influential local leaders in the Maharashtra region.

questions.text: The backbone of the Maratha army was the group of highly mobile peasant-pastoralists called:
questions.explanation: Their mobility and knowledge of the terrain were crucial for guerrilla warfare.

questions.text: The 'Peshwas' were the ________ of the Maratha state.
questions.explanation: The Peshwas became the effective rulers of the Maratha Empire after Shivaji's successors.

questions.text: Which Maratha tax was 25% of the land revenue claimed by zamindars?
questions.explanation: Chauth was collected from non-Maratha territories in exchange for protection from raids.

questions.text: Sardeshmukhi was an additional levy of ________ of the land revenue.
questions.explanation: It was paid to the head revenue collector (Sardeshmukh) in the Deccan.

questions.text: The Marathas were defeated in the Third Battle of Panipat in:
questions.explanation: This defeat halted their expansion towards Delhi and North India.

questions.text: Which Jat leader built the fort at Deeg and established a powerful state around Bharatpur?
questions.explanation: Under Suraj Mal, the Jat state emerged as a strong political power.

questions.text: The city of Hyderabad constanty struggled against the ________ to the west.
questions.explanation: The Marathas were a constant threat to the expansion and stability of Hyderabad.

questions.text: What were the 'Misls' in Sikh history?
questions.explanation: There were 12 misls, each under its own chief, which later united under Ranjit Singh.

questions.text: The Jats were prosperous agriculturists, and towns like ________ became important trading centers.
questions.explanation: The stability provided by the Jat rulers allowed these areas to flourish.

questions.text: The term 'Subadar' refers to:
questions.explanation: Subadars controlled both political and financial administration of a province.

questions.text: Which Mughal Emperor was blinded by his own nobles?
questions.explanation: The later Mughal emperors were often puppets or victims of powerful noble factions.

questions.text: The 'Chauth' and 'Sardeshmukhi' taxes were mainly collected by the:
questions.explanation: These taxes were central to the Maratha financial and military strategy.

questions.text: The capital of the Maratha kingdom was shifted to ________ by the Peshwas.
questions.explanation: Pune became the administrative heart of the Maratha Empire under the Peshwas.

questions.text: Banda Bahadur was captured and executed in:
questions.explanation: He led a major revolt against the Mughals after the death of Guru Gobind Singh.

questions.text: The Marathas developed a successful military organization by avoiding ________.
options: Pitched battles with the Mughal army
questions.explanation: They preferred raiding and hitting the supply lines of the large Mughal armies.

questions.text: Which community occupied the territory between Delhi and Agra during the Mughal decline?
questions.explanation: The Jats consolidated their power in the strategic region near the capital.

questions.text: The title 'Nizam-ul-Mulk' was held by the founder of:
questions.explanation: It literally means 'Administrator of the Realm'.

questions.text: During the 18th century, the Mughals' authority was primarily challenged by:
questions.explanation: A combination of internal and external factors led to the fragmentation of the empire.

questions.text: The 'Peacock Throne' was carried away by:
questions.explanation: It was one of the many treasures looted from Delhi in 1739.

questions.text: The revenue reassessment in Bengal led to many zamindars having to:
options: Borrow money from bankers and moneylenders
questions.explanation: This increased the influence of the merchant-banking class in the state's politics.

questions.text: Maratha expansion into Rajasthan and Bengal happened between:
questions.explanation: This was the period of their greatest territorial expansion.

questions.text: The Sikh territory in the late 18th century extended from:
questions.explanation: They controlled most of the Punjab region before Maharaja Ranjit Singh's reign.

questions.text: The Jats reached their zenith of power under:
questions.explanation: Suraj Mal is remembered as a wise and powerful statesman.

questions.text: By the end of the 18th century, which power was emerging as a major political force in India?
questions.explanation: The political vacuum created by the Mughal decline was eventually filled by the British.
